concept.title: SI Units and Dimensional Analysis
formulas: For Z = A^p B^q/C^r, ΔZ/Z = p(ΔA/A) + q(ΔB/B) + r(ΔC/C)

questions.question: Given the relation $\sqrt{\frac{\hbar G}{c^5}}$, what is its dimension?
questions.explanation: Dimensional analysis: $[\hbar] = ML^2T^{-1}$, $[G] = M^{-1}L^3T^{-2}$, $[c] = LT^{-1}$. So $\frac{\hbar G}{c^5} = \frac{(ML^2T^{-1})(M^{-1}L^3T^{-2})}{(LT^{-1})^5} = T^2$. Square root gives $T$. This is Planck time.

questions.question: Given the relation $\sqrt{\frac{\hbar c}{G}}$, what is its dimension?
questions.explanation: $\frac{\hbar c}{G} = \frac{(ML^2T^{-1})(LT^{-1})}{M^{-1}L^3T^{-2}} = M^2$. Square root gives $M$. This is Planck mass.

questions.question: Given the relation $\sqrt{\frac{\hbar G}{c^3}}$, what is its dimension?
questions.explanation: $\frac{\hbar G}{c^3} = \frac{(ML^2T^{-1})(M^{-1}L^3T^{-2})}{(LT^{-1})^3} = L^2$. Square root gives $L$. This is Planck length.

questions.question: Given that we need to form a quantity with dimension of time using $\hbar$, $c$, and $G$, which relation is correct?
questions.explanation: Only $\sqrt{\hbar G/c^5}$ has dimension of time. This is Planck time $T_P$. Note that $T_P = L_P/c$.

questions.question: Given that we need to form a quantity with dimension of mass using $\hbar$, $c$, and $G$, which relation is correct?
questions.explanation: Only $\sqrt{\hbar c/G}$ has dimension of mass. This is Planck mass $M_P$. It is the mass scale where quantum gravity effects become important.

questions.question: Given that we need to form a quantity with dimension of length using $\hbar$, $c$, and $G$, which relation is correct?
questions.explanation: Only $\sqrt{\hbar G/c^3}$ has dimension of length. This is Planck length $L_P \approx 1.6 \times 10^{-35} \, m$, the scale at which space-time structure becomes quantized.

questions.question: The correct relation of speed, distance and time is........
questions.explanation: Speed is defined as distance covered per unit time: $S = \frac{D}{T}$.

questions.question: An athlete runs a distance of $500 \, m$ in $25s$......... is the average speed of the athlete.
questions.explanation: Average speed = $\frac{Distance}{Time} = \frac{500}{25} = 20 \, m/s = 20 \, ms^{-1}$.

questions.question: A driver drives his car at the constant speed and covers a distance of $288 \, m$ in $60 \, s$. Find the speed
questions.explanation: Speed = $\frac{Distance}{Time} = \frac{288}{60} = 4.8 \, m/s$.

questions.question: When an object is moving at the same speed, what will be its acceleration?
questions.explanation: Same speed in straight line means no change in velocity. Acceleration = 0.

questions.question: Zero acceleration means:
options: The velocity of the object is constant
The velocity of the object is low
The velocity of the object is zero
The velocity of the object increases
questions.explanation: Acceleration = $\frac{dv}{dt}$. If $a = 0$, then $v$ = constant.

questions.question: The velocity of an object is proportional to the time elapsed. The object has:
questions.explanation: If $v \propto t$, then $v = kt$. Acceleration $a = k$ = constant. So uniform acceleration.

questions.question: Acceleration of an object moving at uniform velocity is:
questions.explanation: Uniform velocity means rate of change of velocity = 0. Hence acceleration = zero.

questions.question: An airplane flies at a speed of $50 \, m/s$. How much distance will it cover in $5$ hours?
A truck travels $450 \, km$ in two and a half hours. Find its speed in $m/s$.
questions.explanation: Speed = $\frac{450}{2.5} = 180 \, km/h$. Convert: $180 \times \frac{5}{18} = 50 \, m/s$.

questions.question: A bullet travels $90 \, m$ in $0.2$ seconds. Find its speed in $km/hr$.
questions.explanation: Speed = $\frac{90}{0.2} = 450 \, m/s$. Convert: $450 \times \frac{18}{5} = 1620 \, km/h$.

questions.question: A rocket travels $108 \, m$ in $0.3$ seconds. Find its speed in $km/hr$.
The area of a square field is $313600 \, m^2$. How much time will it take for a woman to cross the field diagonally at the rate of $4\sqrt{2} \, m/s$?
A particle moves with constant acceleration. If initial velocity is $u$ and final velocity is $v$ after time $t$, displacement is:
questions.explanation: Both A and D are correct. For constant acceleration: $s = \frac{u+v}{2} \cdot t$ and $s = ut + \frac{1}{2}at^2$.

questions.question: A car accelerates from rest at $2 \, m/s^2$ for $5 \, s$. Distance covered is:
questions.explanation: Using $s = ut + \frac{1}{2}at^2$ with $u=0$, $s = 0 + \frac{1}{2} \times 2 \times 5^2 = 25 \, m$.

questions.question: If a particle has an initial velocity of $V_0 = 12 \, m/s$ to the right, at $S_0 = 0$, what is the position when $t = 10 \, s$, if $a = 2 \, m/s^2$ to the left?

source.modification_note: Corrected answer from 30 m to 20 m

questions.question: A ball is thrown vertically upward with velocity $20 \, m/s$. Time to reach maximum height is: (take $g = 10 \, m/s^2$)
questions.explanation: At max height, $v = 0$. Using $v = u - gt$, we get $0 = 20 - 10t$ → $t = 2 \, s$.

questions.question: For a body moving with uniform acceleration, the velocity-time graph is a:
options: Straight line parallel to time axis
questions.explanation: From $v = u + at$, v-t graph is straight line with slope = $a$. If $a > 0$, positive slope.

questions.question: Area under acceleration-time graph gives:
questions.explanation: Since $a = dv/dt$, integrating gives $∫a\,dt = Δv$. Area under a-t graph = change in velocity.

questions.question: A boat crosses a river $400 \, m$ wide. Velocity of boat in still water is $5 \, m/s$ and river flows at $3 \, m/s$. Time to cross by shortest path is:
questions.explanation: For shortest path, boat must head upstream at angle. Resultant velocity across river = $\sqrt{5^2 - 3^2} = 4 \, m/s$. Time = $400/4 = 100 \, s$.

questions.question: Two cars A and B move in same direction at $60 \, km/h$ and $80 \, km/h$. Velocity of B w.r.t A is:
questions.explanation: Relative velocity = $v_B - v_A = 80 - 60 = 20 \, km/h$ in direction of motion.

questions.question: Velocity of particle is $v = 6t - 3t^2$. Distance travelled in first $3 \, s$ is:
Which one of the following statement is correct?
options: The speed of an object is always equal to the magnitude of its velocity
The speed of an object can be greater than the magnitude of its velocity
The speed of an object is always less than the magnitude of its velocity
The speed of an object can never be equal to the magnitude of its velocity
questions.explanation: Speed is scalar, total distance/total time. Velocity is vector, displacement/total time. Since distance ≥ |displacement|, speed ≥ |velocity|. For straight line motion without direction change, speed = |velocity|. For any other motion, speed > |velocity|. So speed can be greater than magnitude of velocity. *Rating 2*: Basic definition check.

questions.question:
The velocity-time graph for a particle moving in a straight line is shown. The displacement of the particle in 5 seconds is:

[Graph: Velocity (m/s) on y-axis. From t=0 to t=2s, v=4 m/s. From t=2 to t=4s, v increases linearly to 8 m/s. From t=4 to t=5s, v=8 m/s]
questions.explanation:
Displacement = area under v-t graph. Split into 3 parts:
1. 0-2s: Rectangle, area = 4 × 2 = 8 m
2. 2-4s: Trapezoid, area = ½(4+8)×2 = 12 m 
3. 4-5s: Rectangle, area = 8 × 1 = 8 m
Total = 8 + 12 + 8 = 28 m. *Rating 3*: Graph area calculation.

questions.question: A body starts from rest and moves with uniform acceleration. The ratio of the distance covered in the nth second to the distance covered in n seconds is:
questions.explanation: Distance in n seconds: $s_n = \frac{1}{2}an^2$. Distance in nth second: $s_{nth} = u + \frac{a}{2}(2n-1) = \frac{a}{2}(2n-1)$ since u=0. Ratio = $\frac{\frac{a}{2}(2n-1)}{\frac{1}{2}an^2} = \frac{2n-1}{n^2}$. *Rating 2*: Direct formula application.

questions.question: A stone is dropped from a height h. It hits the ground with a velocity v. If the stone is dropped from a height 4h, the velocity with which it hits the ground is:
questions.explanation: Using $v^2 = u^2 + 2gh$, with u=0. So $v = \sqrt{2gh}$. If height = 4h, then $v' = \sqrt{2g(4h)} = \sqrt{4} × \sqrt{2gh} = 2v$. *Rating 3*: Proportionality in kinematics.

questions.question: Two trains A and B are moving on parallel tracks with velocities 60 km/h and 90 km/h respectively. Train A is ahead of B. The relative velocity of B with respect to A is:
options: 30 km/h opposite to direction of motion
150 km/h opposite to direction of motion
questions.explanation: Relative velocity $v_{BA} = v_B - v_A = 90 - 60 = 30$ km/h. Since B is faster and behind A, B is catching up. So relative velocity is 30 km/h in direction of motion. *Rating 3*: Basic relative velocity.

questions.question: A car accelerates from rest at a constant rate α for some time, after which it decelerates at a constant rate β to come to rest. If the total time elapsed is t, the maximum velocity attained is:
questions.explanation: Let max velocity = $v_{max}$. Time to accelerate: $t_1 = v_{max}/α$. Time to decelerate: $t_2 = v_{max}/β$. Total $t = t_1 + t_2 = v_{max}(1/α + 1/β) = v_{max}(α+β)/αβ$. So $v_{max} = \frac{αβt}{α+β}$. *Rating 4*: Multi-stage motion.

questions.question: The slope of displacement-time graph gives:
questions.explanation: By definition, velocity $v = ds/dt$. On s-t graph, slope = $\Delta s/\Delta t$ = instantaneous velocity. *Rating 3*: Graph interpretation basic.

questions.question: A ball is thrown vertically upward with velocity u. The time taken to reach the maximum height is T. The distance traveled in the last second of its upward journey is:
questions.explanation: At max height v=0, so $T = u/g$. Distance in last second of ascent = distance in 1st second of descent from top = $\frac{1}{2}g(1)^2 = \frac{1}{2}g$. Using symmetry of projectile. *Rating 5*: Requires symmetry concept.

questions.question: A person travels from A to B at a speed of 40 km/h and returns at 60 km/h. The average speed for the whole journey is:
questions.explanation: Let distance AB = d. Time going = d/40, time returning = d/60. Total distance = 2d. Total time = d/40 + d/60 = d(3+2)/120 = 5d/120 = d/24. Average speed = 2d/(d/24) = 48 km/h. Note: not arithmetic mean. *Rating 3*: Harmonic mean trap.

questions.question: A particle moving with uniform acceleration has velocity 6 m/s at a point and 10 m/s at another point which is 64 m away from the first. The acceleration is:
questions.explanation: Using $v^2 = u^2 + 2as$: $10^2 = 6^2 + 2×a×64$ → $100 = 36 + 128a$ → $64 = 128a$ → $a = 0.5$ m/s². *Rating 4*: Direct but needs equation selection.

questions.question: A person moves 30 m north, then 20 m east, then 30√2 m south-west. The displacement from the starting point is:
questions.explanation: Take north=+y, east=+x. Moves: (0,30) → (20,30). Then 30√2 m SW = 30 m west + 30 m south. Final: (20-30, 30-30) = (-10, 0). So 10 m west of start. *Rating 3*: Vector addition.

questions.question: The v-t graph of a particle is a straight line inclined to time axis. The slope of this line represents:
questions.explanation: Slope of v-t graph = $dv/dt$ = acceleration. If line is straight, acceleration is uniform. If line is horizontal, a=0. *Rating 6*: JEE asks this to check graph fundamentals. Common to confuse with s-t graph.

questions.question: Two balls are dropped from heights h and 2h respectively. The ratio of time taken to reach the ground is:
questions.explanation: For free fall from rest, $h = \frac{1}{2}gt^2$ → $t = \sqrt{2h/g}$. So $t ∝ \sqrt{h}$. Ratio = $\sqrt{h} : \sqrt{2h} = 1 : \sqrt{2}$. *Rating 5*: Proportionality trap - not linear.

questions.question: A body starts from rest. If it covers distance $s_1$ in first 10 seconds and $s_2$ in next 10 seconds, then $s_2/s_1$ is:
questions.explanation: $s_1 = \frac{1}{2}a(10)^2 = 50a$. Distance in 20s: $s = \frac{1}{2}a(20)^2 = 200a$. So $s_2 = s - s_1 = 200a - 50a = 150a$. Ratio $s_2/s_1 = 150a/50a = 3$. Or use $s_n ∝ (2n-1)$: 1:3 for n=1,2. *Rating 4*: Tests ratio concept.

questions.question: A police jeep is chasing a thief's car which is moving with velocity 90 km/h. The jeep moving with 126 km/h fires a bullet with muzzle velocity 360 km/h. The bullet hits the car with velocity:
questions.explanation: Velocity of bullet w.r.t ground = velocity of jeep + muzzle velocity = 126 + 360 = 486 km/h. Velocity of car = 90 km/h. Relative velocity of bullet w.r.t car = 486 - 90 = 396 km/h. *Rating 5*: Double relative motion. JEE loves this type.

questions.question: If $\vec{A} = 3\hat{i} + 4\hat{j}$ and $\vec{B} = \hat{i} - 2\hat{j}$, magnitude of $\vec{A} + \vec{B}$ is:
questions.explanation: $\vec{A} + \vec{B} = 4\hat{i} + 2\hat{j}$. Magnitude = $\sqrt{4^2 + 2^2} = \sqrt{20} = 2\sqrt{5}$.

questions.question: Angle between $\vec{A} = \hat{i} + \hat{j}$ and $\vec{B} = \hat{i} - \hat{j}$ is:
questions.explanation: Dot product $\vec{A}·\vec{B} = (1)(1) + (1)(-1) = 0$. If dot product = 0, vectors are perpendicular: $90^\circ$.

questions.question: For a projectile, range is maximum when angle of projection is:
questions.explanation: Range $R = \frac{u^2 \sin2\theta}{g}$. $R_{max}$ when $\sin2\theta = 1$ → $2\theta = 90^\circ$ → $\theta = 45^\circ$.

questions.question: In uniform circular motion, the acceleration is directed:
questions.explanation: Centripetal acceleration $a_c = \frac{v^2}{r}$ is always directed towards the center of the circle, perpendicular to velocity.

options: Graph (a): $a$ vs $r$ is a horizontal line
Graph (b): $a$ vs $r$ is a straight line with positive slope
Graph (d): $a$ vs $r$ is a rectangular hyperbola, decreasing with $r$
questions.explanation: For uniform circular motion, centripetal acceleration $a = \frac{v^2}{r}$. Since $v$ is constant, $a \propto \frac{1}{r}$. This is a rectangular hyperbola. As $r$ increases, $a$ decreases. Graph (d) shows $a$ decreasing with $r$ like $1/r$. **Rating 4**: Graph interpretation + $a_c$ formula. 1.5m.

options.label: (a)
options.code: \begin{tikzpicture}\begin{axis}[axis lines = left, xlabel = {$r$}, ylabel = {$a$}, xtick = \empty, ytick = \empty, xmin=0, xmax=4, ymin=0, ymax=3, width=5cm, height=4cm]\addplot[black, thick] coordinates {(0,2) (4,2)};\end{axis}\end{tikzpicture}

options.label: (b)
options.code: \begin{tikzpicture}\begin{axis}[axis lines = left, xlabel = {$r$}, ylabel = {$a$}, xtick = \empty, ytick = \empty, xmin=0, xmax=4, ymin=0, ymax=4, width=5cm, height=4cm]\addplot[black, thick] {0.5 + 0.8*x};\end{axis}\end{tikzpicture}

options.label: (c)
options.code: \begin{tikzpicture}\begin{axis}[axis lines = left, xlabel = {$r$}, ylabel = {$a$}, xtick = \empty, ytick = \empty, xmin=0, xmax=3, ymin=0, ymax=4, width=5cm, height=4cm, domain=0:2.5]\addplot[black, thick] {0.5*x^2};\end{axis}\end{tikzpicture}

options.label: (d)
options.code: \begin{tikzpicture}\begin{axis}[axis lines = left, xlabel = {$r$}, ylabel = {$a$}, xtick = \empty, ytick = \empty, xmin=0, xmax=5, ymin=0, ymax=5, width=5cm, height=4cm, domain=0.5:4.5]\addplot[black, thick] {4/x};\end{axis}\end{tikzpicture}

concept.title: Newton's Laws of Motion
body: Newton's **First Law** (Law of Inertia) states that a body remains in a state of rest or uniform motion in a straight line unless acted upon by an external unbalanced force.

Newton's **Second Law** relates force, mass, and acceleration: $$F = ma$$ where $F$ is the net force (N), $m$ is mass (kg), and $a$ is acceleration (m/s²). This is the most widely used law for solving dynamics problems.

Newton's **Third Law** states that for every action there is an equal and opposite reaction: $$\vec{F}_{AB} = -\vec{F}_{BA}$$ These action-reaction pairs always act on different bodies, so they never cancel each other out.

Key application — inclined planes: For a frictionless incline at angle $\theta$, the component of gravity along the incline is $mg\sin\theta$, giving acceleration $a = g\sin\theta$. The normal force is $N = mg\cos\theta$.
Free body diagrams are essential: always resolve forces into perpendicular components before applying $F=ma$ along each axis.

questions.question: A body of mass $m$ is placed on a frictionless incline of angle $\theta$. The acceleration of the body along the incline is:
questions.explanation: On a frictionless incline, the component of gravity along the surface is $mg\sin\theta$, so $a = g\sin\theta$.

questions.question: Which of the following are correct statements about Newton's Third Law? (Select all that apply)
options: Action and reaction forces act on different bodies
Action and reaction forces are equal in magnitude
Action and reaction forces cancel each other
Action and reaction are opposite in direction
questions.explanation: Newton's Third Law: action-reaction pairs act on different bodies, are equal in magnitude and opposite in direction. They do NOT cancel because they act on different bodies.

questions.question: A force $\vec{F} = 10\hat{i} + 5\hat{j}$ N acts on a 2 kg body. The magnitude of acceleration is:
A block of mass $4\,\text{kg}$ is pulled by a horizontal force of $20\,\text{N}$ on a frictionless surface. Find its acceleration in m/s².
questions.explanation: Using $F = ma$: $a = F/m = 20/4 = 5$ m/s².

concept.title: Work, Energy & Power
body: Work done by a force is the dot product of force and displacement: $$W = \vec{F} \cdot \vec{d} = |F||d|\cos\theta$$ Work is a scalar quantity measured in Joules (J).

Kinetic Energy is the energy possessed by a body due to its motion: $$KE = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$$ The Work-Energy Theorem states that the net work done on a body equals its change in kinetic energy: $W_{net} = \Delta KE$.

Conservative forces (gravity, spring/elastic force) do work that is independent of the path taken — only initial and final positions matter. Their work over a closed path is zero. Non-conservative forces (friction, air drag) dissipate energy as heat and depend on the path.
Potential Energy is associated with conservative forces. Gravitational PE: $U = mgh$. Spring PE: $U = \frac{1}{2}kx^2$.
Power is the rate of doing work: $$P = \frac{W}{t} = \vec{F}\cdot\vec{v}$$ measured in Watts (W).
Conservation of Mechanical Energy: In the absence of non-conservative forces, total mechanical energy ($KE + PE$) remains constant.

questions.question: The work done by a force $\vec{F}$ over displacement $\vec{d}$ is given by:
questions.explanation: Work is the dot product: $W = \vec{F} \cdot \vec{d} = |F||d|\cos\theta$.

questions.question: Which of the following are conservative forces? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: Conservative forces (gravity, spring) have work independent of path. Friction and drag are non-conservative.

questions.question: A $2\,\text{kg}$ object moving at $3\,\text{m/s}$ speeds up to $5\,\text{m/s}$. Find the work done on it (in Joules).

units.title: Unit 7: Properties of Solids and Liquids

concept.title: Ideal Gas Equation, Degrees of Freedom
Wave Motion Basics
body: A wave is a disturbance that transfers energy through a medium (or vacuum, for EM waves) without net transport of matter.
A travelling wave can be written as $$y(x,t) = A\sin(kx - \omega t)$$ where $A$ is amplitude, $k = 2\pi/\lambda$ is the wave number, and $\omega = 2\pi f$ is the angular frequency.
Wave speed relates frequency and wavelength: $$v = f\lambda = \frac{\omega}{k}$$
Transverse waves (e.g. light, waves on a string) have particle displacement perpendicular to wave propagation. Longitudinal waves (e.g. sound) have displacement parallel to propagation.

The principle of superposition states that when two or more waves overlap, the resultant displacement is the algebraic sum of individual displacements — this underlies interference and standing waves.

questions.question: The equation of a wave is $y = A\sin(kx - \omega t)$. The speed of the wave is:
questions.explanation: Wave speed $v = \frac{\omega}{k} = f\lambda$.

questions.question: A wave has frequency $50\,\text{Hz}$ and wavelength $4\,\text{m}$. Find its speed (in m/s).
questions.explanation: $v = f\lambda = 50 \times 4 = 200$ m/s.
